title: Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Net](#dod:net-oda) [official development assistance (ODA)](#dod:oda), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.
caption:
Foreign aid given per capita vs. GDP per capita
Net official development assistance (ODA), expressed in US dollars and adjusted for inflation.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and foreign aid contributions, plotting GDP per capita on the horizontal axis against net official development assistance given per capita on the vertical axis. The horizontal axis ranges from zero to over $120,000 in 2021 international dollars, while the vertical axis measures aid in constant 2023 US dollars up to $800. Circles represent individual countries, sized according to population, with colors distinguishing regions including North America, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A general positive trend is visible where countries with higher GDP per capita tend to give more aid per person. Norway sits at the highest point on the vertical axis, exceeding $800 in aid per capita with a GDP per capita near $90,000. Luxembourg and Ireland also appear in the upper right quadrant, with Ireland positioned near $120,000 GDP per capita and roughly $450 in aid. Switzerland and Sweden occupy the middle-upper range, both showing aid levels between $400 and $500. The United States appears as a large circle due to its population size, positioned around $80,000 GDP per capita and just under $200 in aid per capita. Qatar stands apart on the far right with a GDP per capita exceeding $110,000 but aid giving near $250. Asian nations like Japan, South Korea, and Turkey cluster in the lower-left to middle section, with GDP per capita between $30,000 and $50,000 and aid levels generally below $200. Thailand and Kazakhstan sit near the bottom left with lower GDP and minimal aid per capita. The data shows that while wealthier nations generally contribute more per person, the magnitude of that contribution varies significantly across high-income economies.
